Home»Sport»Paris Olympics 2024»Why Simbine will earn almost triple if he wins Olympics gold

Akani Simbine could earn more than triple the amount for a gold medal compared to other Team SA athletes at the Paris Olympics. Here is why.

As reported this week, Tatjana Smith isset to pocketR400 000after claiming gold at the Paris Olympics on Monday, thanks to the financial incentives guaranteed by theSouth African Sports Confederation and Olympic Committee (Sascoc).

Smith, formerly known as Schoenmaker, will be back in action in thewomen’s 200m breaststroke, and so she could quite possibly double those earnings as she will once again be the favourite for gold in that event.

Similarly,Akani Simbineis one of the favourites in the men’s 100m sprint, and there is a massive extra incentive that does make for interesting reading.

Earlier this year,World Athleticsannouncedit will become the first international federation to award prize money at anOlympics. Gold medal winners in each of the 48 athletics events in Paris will receive$50 000 (R915 000).

The total prize fund of $2.4 million will come from the International Olympic Committee’s revenue share allocation that World Athletics receives every four years. Each individual Olympic champion will receive $50 000 and relay teams will receive the same amount, to be shared among the team members.

Along with the Team SA bonus of R400 000, if Simbine could also clinch the R915 000 up for grabs from World Athletics, he could ultimately bank just over R1.3 million if he grabs gold. That would be more than triple any other single gold medal winner from Team SA who aren’t part of the athletics team.

Anything less than gold, though, and the athletics duo will only be in line for the Sascoc incentives as World Athletics prize money will only be paid to the winners of silver and bronze medals from the 2028 Olympics in Los Angeles.

World Athletics President Sebastian Coe said in a statement: “The introduction of prize money for Olympic gold medallists is a pivotal moment for World Athletics and the sport of athletics as a whole, underscoring our commitment to empowering the athletes and recognising the critical role they play in the success of any Olympic Games.

“We are now in a position to also fund gold medal performances for athletes in Paris, with a commitment to reward all three medallists at the LA28 Olympic Games.

“This is the continuation of a journey we started back in 2015, which sees all the money World Athletics receives from the International Olympic Committee for the Olympic Games go directly back into our sport.”

IndividualsGold medallists R400 000 and the coach R100 000Silver medallists R200 000 and the coach R50 000Bronze medallists R75 000 and the coach R25 000

title: Why Simbine will earn almost triple if he wins Olympics gold
author: Craig Lewis
